text: Namaskaram. Welcome. Often I think about how hard life is, how many challenges each of
us must face on this path. And then I think about those of us on
the spiritual path and how in the beginning it feels It's so difficult to make
sense of the prarabdha karma and how the prarabdha karma has led us to the
spiritual path, and the layers of inner work one has to do to reconcile our
actions, thoughts, and deeds on the level of prarabdha karma as we come into spiritual
awareness, and that is not easy either. And my conclusion is it is time to
stop expecting life and things to be easy, especially when we're not putting in the
real work. And I don't think life was meant to be easy, but life is
and was meant to be this beautiful journey between Leela, Maya, Karma, and Moksha. Leela
is this divine play where we know that we are part of this Divine Mother's
grace and her system of life as we travel through Tamas, Rajas, and Sattva. And
maya is an essential part of the tamas, rajas, sattva experience. And we will be
worked hard through tamas, rajas, and even sattva, through internal forces and through external forces
such as family members, relationships, finances, health. Mental health, and so on and so forth.
One of my personal secrets is that I'm willing to do whatever I need to
do in order to make this incarnation as magnificent as possible. And I wasn't born
with this awareness. I wasn't born with this sankalpa. It's something I've had to work
on after a lot of suffering, a lot of ignorance and pain. And I'm choosing
to live my life in such a way that I speak and live from the
highest integrity and truth. I love as deeply as I can, and I have as
much compassion as possible for all people, and I laugh as much as I can.
And more recently, I rest as much as I need when the body gets weary
and the mind gets tired. I know that as I journey forward beyond this lifetime,
I would like to take all the wisdom, all the blessings that I have received
in this lifetime through my personal work and all the grace of Divine Mother onward
to my next journey. So my next journey will begin with more awareness, more sattva,
more grace. And when we look at ourselves from the lens of the soul, as
in first and foremost I'm a soul, and secondly I'm the soul within me is
creating, guiding, and having this human experience, and what I do in each lifetime impacts
the next and the next and the next and beyond, then at least for me,
I'm invested in how I am in this lifetime, knowing it will impact me in
the next and the one after and beyond. And the decision to live the way
I have chosen to live after my beautiful transformations is not an investment in future
lifetimes. It's actually an investment here and now. It just so happens that it impacts
the future as well. I have this deep knowing that we are here to share
our light with the world. We're here to give to this world. We're here to
be the expression of the divine in these bodies with these names and identities. And
how we do it is dependent on that prarabdha karma that we brought with us,
that slice of karma that applies to all that we're going to experience in this
lifetime. And one thing that I have found to be very helpful for myself is
to let go of victimhood. And not feel sorry for myself for the challenges I've
faced or the losses I've endured, etc. And when we begin to realize that this
is a very specific set of karmas that I brought here to walk through the
maya of it and into the leela of it Till I come to that place
where Divine Mother grabs my hand and says, oh, you've done so well, come, let
me shepherd you to the side of moksha. Moksha means liberation, but not— I'm not
talking about liberation from this body. I'm talking about liberation in this body, in this
lifetime. Liberation means that I am not completely tethered and attached to my patterns, my
karmas. I'm willing to be humble. I'm willing to let go. I'm willing to surrender
to what I'm really here to do, which is not suffering. And most often we
come to that from and through suffering. A lot of paradoxes and a lot of
polarities to our experiences here. So my question for all of us is, what do
you want to do with the karmas you've brought into this life? I will be
making a video on what karma really means, not all the things that people say
in America. I'll share the real meaning of karma. So what are you here to
do based on what you've endured, what you've experienced? And are you doing it? And
are you able to shift out of that victimhood into, wow, as I overcome my
personal challenges, I have nothing but great, beautiful things to give to this world. Whether
you're a farmer or a scientist or a teacher or a mother or anything else.
And are you doing it? And By knowing that that's what you're here to do,
can you derive more enjoyment and fulfillment from doing it? So that is my sweet
little invitation to you. Thank you for being here and sharing your precious time with
me. Namaskaram. Om Namah Shivaya. Shri Matare Namaha. Om Shri Guru Bhyo Namaha.
